data.title: ManpowerGroup: Still Being Patient On This Stock
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on ManpowerGroup Inc. (MAN) was a hold rating because profitability remained weak despite organic growth showing two straight quarters of recovery. After looking at the Q1 2026 results, I still think a hold rating makes sense. The better part of this quarter was that the revenue recovery looks more credible now, with improvement across France, Italy, Southern Europe, and the Manpower brand. That said, the earnings side still does not look clean enough. Gross profit fell, gross margin missed guidance, and the strongest demand is still coming from lower-margin enterprise customers. My fear is that consensus estimates are too high and MAN misses expectations, which would very likely weigh on the stock valuation.

1Q26 results update

MAN saw Q1 2026 revenue of ~$4.51 billion, up 10.3% y/y on a reported basis. On a constant-currency basis, revenue grew 2.9% y/y. Note that organic days-adjusted constant-currency revenue grew 3% in the quarter, and systemwide revenue, which includes franchise revenue, was $5 billion.

By geography, Americas revenue saw $1.12 billion, up 3.5% y/y in constant currency. Within that, US revenue came in at $655 million, down 5% on a days-adjusted basis. Southern Europe revenue saw $2.1 billion, up 3% y/y in constant currency; France revenue saw $1.1 billion and was flat in constant currency, while Italy revenue was $475 million and was up 8% on a days-adjusted constant-currency basis. Northern Europe revenue was $790 million, down 1% in organic constant currency. Finally, Asia Pacific Middle East revenue saw $510.5 million, up 8.1% y/y in constant currency, and within that, Japan revenue grew 4% on a days-adjusted constant-currency basis. By brand, Manpower revenue grew 6% y/y in organic constant currency, up from 5% growth in Q4 2025. Experis revenue declined 9% y/y, worse than the 6% decline in Q4 2025, mainly due to the timing of healthcare IT projects in the US. Talent Solutions revenue declined 1% y/y, which was better than the 4% decline in Q4 2025.

As for profitability, gross profit saw $723 million, up 3.5% y/y on a reported basis but down 2.8% in constant currency. Margin-wise, gross margin was 16%, down ~100 bps y/y, with Staffing margin reducing gross margin by 70 bps due to mix shifts and bench utilization, permanent recruitment reducing margin by 20 bps, and other services reducing margin by another 20 bps. The good news was that adj. SG&A fell 4% y/y in constant currency and now represents ~15% of revenue, down from ~16% in Q1 2025. All in all, this led to net earnings of $2.5 million, or $0.05 per diluted share on a GAAP basis. But adjusting for restructuring and strategic transformation program costs, adjusted EPS was $0.51.

The revenue recovery story is getting more credible

A few months ago, the main question I had was whether MAN had simply stopped getting worse or whether a real turnaround had started. I think this Q1 2026 performance made it clear that MAN is turning around. Q1 marked the fifth straight quarter of year-over-year revenue trend improvement for organic days-adjusted constant-currency revenue (up 3%). What's important is where the improvement came from. France finally got back to flat (was down 2% in Q4 2025); Italy's growth accelerated to 8%; and Southern Europe continued to print positive growth of 3%. Management also said manufacturing conditions are getting better across Europe and the US, with improving PMI trends and good momentum in countries like Italy and Spain. The numbers also suggest demand momentum is improving, as the Manpower brand (mainly for industrial and manufacturing hiring) saw growth acceleration sequentially, by 100 bps to 6%. Broadly, the idea here is that recovery is showing up across several places, and that makes the growth setup much more attractive.

I should also add that the strongest demand seen in Q1 came from enterprise customers. I view this very positively because large clients usually move first when hiring conditions start to improve. So, while the mix is not ideal for margin, it is still a useful signal that demand is coming back. We have actually seen this happening in Q1, and now with two quarters of this happening, I believe there is real hiring activity that is backing this recovery story. Importantly, management said pricing remains rational, which suggests MAN is not buying growth through weaker pricing.

There is also a second leg to the story now. MAN is structurally lowering its cost base. So far, work has started from the back office, which I expect to help remove some overhead and standardize support work, which means some savings can start showing up in 2026. The front-office redesign is probably more important because it impacts how MAN actually sells, recruits, and serves clients. My view is that if recruiters can source, screen, and match candidates faster, and if sales teams can target demand better, MAN should be able to support more revenue without growing headcount by as much. A good example is the AI sales targeting tool in France, which has already generated ~$200 million of incremental revenue. MAN has also completed >25,000 AI-led interviews in the past six months and reduced screening time by 67%. If this goes on, it should naturally lead to margin expansion, which solves the concern I had about profitability.

Valuation

The valuation looks optically cheap vs. peers and its history, but I think that may be misleading if consensus is too aggressive on adj. EPS. Consensus is already pricing in an acceleration in adj. EPS growth, as you can see in the table below. Yes, the revenue growth trend is improving, and that has helped sentiment. But I am not confident that adj. EPS will grow as fast as consensus expects. The reality is gross profit fell despite positive organic growth, gross margin missed guidance, the enterprise mix is still weighing on profitability (despite this being a topline contributor), and permanent hiring remains soft.

I would prefer to wait for another 1 or 2 quarters of MAN showing strong adj. EPS growth before I'm convinced that the topline growth story is translating to earnings growth.

Conclusion

My view on MAN is still a hold rating. I can see why sentiment has improved, because the top-line recovery is getting broader and management is also pushing on cost actions and productivity tools. But I am worried consensus estimates may be too high, as the enterprise mix is still weighing on margins, Experis remains weak, and permanent hiring has not improved enough. I may miss the first leg of share price recovery if I am wrong, but I prefer to wait for another 1 to 2 quarters of similar/better adj. EPS growth before I turn more bullish.

data.title: Credo: The Market Hasn't Fully Priced In The DustPhotonics Deal's Implications
data.description:
Performance assessment

Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO) has risen 41% in less than 2 weeks. It looks like my 'Strong Buy' upgrade was very timely:

Elevator pitch

In my last article on Credo, I noted how the company is foraying into the semiconductor optics/photonics market via its ZeroFlap (ZF) Optics product. Just 1 week later, Credo announced a major M&A deal that doubles down on this new product category bet. In this piece, I will analyze this deal and its implications for Credo:

  1. The DustPhotonics acquisition can boost Credo's revenues and margins.
  2. The deal is at an attractive transaction multiple.
  3. Credo's balance sheet can easily handle the upfront cash consideration.
  4. The relative valuation discount has narrowed but is still below the comps median.
  5. CRDO stock may take a breather here.

The DustPhotonics acquisition can boost Credo's revenues and margins

DustPhotonics produces Silicon Photonics Photonic Integrated Circuits (SiPho PICs). Let's break that down:

  • Silicon photonics means it uses light (photon particles) to move and process information on silicon chips. This is similar in concept to fiber optic cables, which move data around using light instead of copper wires.
  • Photonic integrated circuits (PICs) are the optical version of the electronic circuit.

SiPho PICs are used in optical transceivers, which convert between electrical signals and light signals in circuits. This is how it fits into Credo's optical components portfolio:

Credo's ZF Optics module (discussed in more depth in my last article) requires 2 key components:

  1. An optical digital signal processor (DSP) that generates and cleans up electrical signals.
  2. A PIC that converts between electrical and optical signals.

Credo already has the DSP capability in-house. But it currently buys PICs from other vendors. With the DustPhotonics acquisition, Credo not only adds a merchant PIC business from the sale of those PICs but also enables them to sell a more vertically integrated ZF Optics product. Overall, this is expected to lead to an overall optics revenue contribution of $500M in FY27 and an overall high revenue growth rate too.

Our fiscal '27 revenue is now expected to grow in excess of 75% year-over-year, and that's with a mid-single-digit sequential growth through the first half of the year and the second half inflection driven by that ramp of our optical portfolio, which is optical DSP, ZF optics, and now, our silicon photonics platform.

- CFO Daniel Fleming in the DustPhotonics M&A call.

Note that Credo has an April-ending fiscal year.

The Apr'26e number is formed by considering Credo's Q4 FY26 revenue guidance of $430M.

Note that the 75% YoY growth management is indicating for FY27 is not yet reflected in consensus revenue estimates, which are still penciling in 53%:

Hence, I see a case for revenue upside here.

I also expect the in-sourcing of the PIC capability to help reduce component costs:

Critically, SiPho PIC technology is a foundational component of Credo's ZeroFlap (ZF) Optical Transceiver platform. Bringing this capability in-house mitigates external supply dependencies, accelerates product development cycles, and creates a pathway to substantial cost structure improvement at volume.

- Credo M&A Press Release.

This can lead to an uptick in gross margins over the medium term to ~70% levels in my view:

The April 26 gross margin of 65% is based on management's guidance.

The deal is at an attractive transaction multiple

Credo will acquire DustPhotonics for upfront consideration of $750 million cash and approximately 0.92 million shares of Credo common stock... In addition, Credo may pay incremental contingent consideration of up to approximately 3.21 million shares based on the achievement of certain financial milestones.

- Credo Press Release.

Based on this, assuming the share price at the deal announcement on 13 Apr'26 of $134.64 and the current share price of $160.69 for the contingent shares consideration, I arrive at an EV consideration range of $874M—$1.39B.

Now, Credo's optical business before this acquisition was expected to contribute $300M in revenues in FY27. And this M&A deal is expected to lead to optical revenues of more than $500M in FY27. So based on this, I compute an implied EV/revenue multiple of 4.4-6.9x:

To see how this deal multiple compares, I have put together a short list of relevant transaction comps from Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL)—Inphi, Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO)—Acacia, Coherent Corp. (COHR)—II-VI, and Intel Corporation (INTC)—Tower Semiconductor Ltd. (TSEM) deals using company filings, press releases, and data from Capital IQ. I have also considered the relevant semiconductors' revenue multiple average based on data from FirstPageSage:

Putting this all together, I estimate that Credo's DustPhotonics acquisition has occurred at a 30.2% discount based on upfront consideration numbers and an 11% premium based on potential total consideration numbers:

The Intel-Tower Semiconductor deal never went through as it did not get the requisite regulatory approvals; however, I have still included it as I view it as a key information signal since there was no issue with the valuations.

Overall, I conclude that the DustPhotonics M&A is happening at an attractive transaction multiple. I am giving far more weight to the upfront consideration numbers, as the potential total consideration is likely to overstate the price paid, as it does not account for the additional revenue milestones that would be hit to activate that scenario. That detail is not disclosed publicly right now.

Credo's balance sheet can easily handle the upfront cash consideration

Before this deal announcement, Credo had amassed a good deal of liquidity over the past 2 quarters. So it has an ample amount remaining after the $750M upfront cash consideration outflow:

I wonder if the accumulation of total liquidity may be a leading indicator of an upcoming M&A deal for Credo. More data is needed to get a more reliable signal, but I think this means we should have the company's liquidity levels as a key monitorable going ahead.

The company's balance sheet remains very robust, as it will remain in a $454M net cash position even after this deal.

This allows the company to have the resources to continue funding growth investments itself and also bear some cyclicality.

The relative valuation discount narrowed but is still below the comps median

CRDO's 1-yr forward P/E of 36.3x is now at a 21.6% discount vs. the 46.3x median of its peers. This is a smaller relative valuation discount than 35.7% almost 2 weeks ago. So the market is clearly re-rating the stock.

To me, CRDO's valuation still seems attractive as it is still cheaper than many of its comps.

CRDO stock may take a breather here

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using technical analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

CRDO Technical Analysis

CRDO has moved up very sharply over the last couple of weeks with impressive bullish momentum. Now, the stock has reached a key resistance area from which the sellers printed a bearish engulfing as they pushed the stock down. So I am a little bit more cautious here. I expect a bit of a pause here during the broader rally up.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish, as the purchase consideration for DustPhotonics is quite reasonable, and this business can boost both revenues and margins for Credo.
  • Valuation: Bullish, as Credo is still trading at a valuation discount compared to its comps.
  • Technical Analysis: Neutral as the bullish momentum faces a key resistance level.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, Strong Buy.
